Question Text: Two populations of birds with somewhat different coloration live on opposite sides of a peninsula. The habitat between the populations is not suitable for these birds. When birds from the two populations are brought together they produce young whose appearance is intermediate between the two parents. These offspring will breed with each other or with birds from either parent population and all offspring of these pairings appear intermediate to various degrees. What keeps the two populations separate?
Option 1: Temporal reproductive isolation
Option 3: Gametic barrier

Question Text: The two populations [described in Question 1] are _______.
Option 1: Different subspecies under the morphospecies concept.
Option 2: Different species under the biological species concept.
Option 3: Different species under the phylogenetic species concept.

Question Text: Three populations of crickets look very similar but the males have courtship songs that sound different. What function would this difference in song likely serve if the populations came in contact?
Option 1: A prezygotic isolating mechanism
Option 3: All of the above

Question Text: Two hypothetical species of sparrow A and B overwinter together in mixed flocks in Costa Rica. In spring Species A goes to the east coast of North America and Species B goes to the west coast. What can you say about the isolating mechanisms of these two species?
Option 1: They must have strong prezygotic or postzygotic isolating mechanisms in order to spend winter in such close proximity.
Option 2: The two species do not breed in the same area so they are reproductively isolated by allopatry.
Option 3: One of the species is probably polyploid so they are reproductively isolated by genetic incompatibility.
Option 4: Reinforcement must be occurring when they winter together.

Question Text: Refer to Figure 26.1. Which of the following forms a monophyletic group?
Option 1: A, B, C, D
Option 3: D, E, and F

Question Text: The peppered moth... The light-colored form was predominant... In the mid-nineteenth century a dark-colored form appeared... Recently use of cleaner fuels has greatly reduced soot... and the dark-colored moths have been disappearing. Should the two forms of moths be considered separate species?
Option 1: Yes because natural selection has affected the frequency of the two different forms.
Option 2: Yes because they have completely different coloration.
Option 3: No because there is no evidence that they are reproductively isolated.
Option 4: Both A and B are correct.

Question Text: The sister populations [of snapping shrimp] on opposite sides of the Isthmus of Panama are true species under which species concept?
Option 1: The morphospecies concept
Option 3: The phylogenetic species concept

Question Text: If the Isthmus formed gradually rather than suddenly what pattern of genetic divergence would you expect to find in these species pairs?
Option 1: Similar percentages of difference in DNA sequence between all pairs of sister species
Option 2: Greater percentage of difference in DNA sequence between species that inhabit deep water than between species that inhabit shallow water
Option 3: Greater percentage of difference in DNA sequence between species that inhabit shallow water than between species that inhabit deep water

Question Text: Which of the following describes the most likely order of events in speciation?
Option 1: Genetic drift, genetic isolation, divergence
Option 3: Divergence, genetic drift, genetic isolation

Question Text: You want to study divergence of populations... You will form a new population by taking some individuals from a source population and isolating them. What combination of characteristics would maximize your chance of seeing divergence in this study? (1. Random sample 2. Extreme individuals 3. Many offspring 4. Few large offspring 5. Same environment 6. Novel environment)
When the Isthmus of Panama formed a land bridge resulted between North and South America. What opportunities for colonization did this land bridge offer?
Option 1: Colonization of the newly arisen land by organisms from North and South America
Option 2: Colonization of North America by organisms from South America
Option 3: Colonization of South America by organisms from North America

Question Text: The snake family Typhlopidae consists of small burrowing species with vestigial eyes. They are found in Australia Sub-Saharan Africa India and some adjacent areas and South America. What is the most likely explanation for this distribution?
Option 1: Rafting to the different continents from the point of origin on one of these continents
Option 2: Convergent evolution in the different regions
Option 3: Origin on Gondwana followed by continental drift and some range expansion

Question Text: How are two different species most likely to evolve from one ancestral species?
Option 1: Sympatrically, by a point mutation affecting morphology or behavior
Option 2: Allopatrically, after the ancestral species has split into two populations
Option 3: Phylogenetically, due to heterozygote advantage in hybrids

Question Text: Which of the following could be a vicariance event for species in that habitat?
Option 1: The level of water in a lake recedes creating two lakes where there used to be one.
Option 2: Some insects get blown in a storm to a new mountain range where they lay eggs.
Option 3: Radiation near Chernobyl increases mutation rate causing an increase in autopolyploidy.
Option 4: All of the above could be vicariance events for species in that habitat.

Question Text: Which of the following does not tend to promote speciation?
Option 1: The founder effect
Option 3: Natural selection

Question Text: House finches... were found only in Western North America until 1939... If the two forms [Western and New York] were unable to interbreed when their expanding ranges meet what would this situation illustrate?
Option 1: Vicariance
Option 3: Autopolyploidy

Question Text: Most causes of speciation are relatively slow in that they may take many generations of organism to see changes EXCEPT _______.
Option 1: Polyploidy
Option 3: Colonization

Question Text: Two researchers experimentally formed tetraploid frogs... When they mated these tetraploid frogs with each other most of the offspring that survived to maturity were tetraploid... If this type of tetraploid formed in the wild what would be the result?
Option 1: The two parent species would interbreed and fuse into one species.
Option 2: The two parent species would recognize each other as mates.
Option 3: The tetraploids would be reproductively isolated from both parent species.
Option 4: The tetraploids would be selected against.
Option 5: All of the above answers are correct.

Question Text: Why is speciation by polyploidy more likely in plants than in animals?
Option 1: Plant gametes can be produced from somatic cells that have undergone many rounds of mitosis.
Option 2: Plant gametes lack postzygotic isolating mechanisms.
Option 3: Plants lack the DNA repair enzymes that animals have.
Option 4: Plants are sessile and cannot speciate via dispersal.

Question Text: Which of the following statements explains why animals are less likely than plants to speciate by polyploidy?
Option 1: Animals self-fertilize less often than plants, so diploid gametes are less likely to fuse.
Option 2: Animals have better mechanisms for repairing chromosomes than plants have.
Option 3: Animals are better at recognizing appropriate mates.
Option 4: Animals are more mobile, so populations get separated far less often.

Question Text: If a plant species with 2n = 14 forms an allopolyploid with a plant species with 2n = 18 what would be the likely diploid (2n) number of the allopolyploid?
A small number of birds arrive on an island from a neighboring larger island... About twice a year one or two more birds from the neighboring island arrive. What effect do these new arrivals have?
Option 1: Their arrival speeds the process of speciation.
Option 2: Their arrival tends to promote adaptation to the new food plants.
Option 3: Their arrival tends to retard adaptation to the new food plants.
Option 4: Their arrival represents a colonizing event.

Question Text: A researcher notices that in a certain moth species some females prefer to feed and lay eggs on domesticated solanaceous plants... Other females prefer to feed and lay eggs on wild solanaceous plants... What might be occurring in this moth species?
Option 1: Divergence in sympatry
Option 3: Postzygotic isolation

Question Text: You are studying lizards in the field... You find hybrids in the zone of overlap. What is happening?
Option 1: Reinforcement is occurring.
Option 2: The postzygotic isolating mechanisms aren't working.
Option 3: Hybrids are best adapted to this region where overlap occurs.
Option 4: There is not enough information to decide what is happening.

Question Text: A storm brings two formerly separated populations of beetles together... Under the biological species concept which of the following would show that the two populations are different species?
Option 1: One population breeds in spring, the other in fall.
Option 2: Males of the two populations have different flight patterns in courtship.
Option 3: When individuals from the two populations mate with each other in the laboratory, the eggs fail to hatch.
Option 4: All of the above are correct.
Option 5: None of the above are correct.

Question Text: The common edible frog of Europe is a hybrid... Both male and female hybrids exist but when they mate among themselves they are rarely successful in producing offspring. What can you infer from this information?
Option 1: Postzygotic isolation exists between the two frog species.
Option 2: Prezygotic isolation exists between the two frog species.
Option 3: These two species are likely in the process of fusing back into one species.
Option 4: The hybrids form a separate species under the biological species concept.

Question Text: Male frogs give calls... If reinforcement of prezygotic isolation is occurring what would you expect if you compare the calls of the two species in zones of sympatry versus zones of allopatry?
Option 1: Calls would be about the same in both areas.
Option 2: Calls would be more similar in areas of sympatry.
Option 3: Calls would be more different in areas of sympatry.
Option 4: There is not enough information to decide what is happening.

Question Text: Male frogs give calls... What outcomes can occur where the ranges of two species overlap?
Option 1: The species will interbreed, eventually fusing over time.
Option 2: A stable hybrid zone will form if hybrids are better adapted to the area of overlap than either parent species is.
Option 3: Species will continue to diverge and be isolated by behavioral or genetic mechanisms.
Option 4: All of the above are possible outcomes.
Option 5: None of the above are a possible outcome.

Question Text: Two species of tree frogs that live sympatrically in the Northeastern United States differ in ploidy... The frogs are identical in appearance but their mating calls... differ. Which difference most likely evolved first?
Option 1: Polyploidy
Option 3: A and B must have occurred at the same time.

Question Text: Natural selection for traits that keep distinct populations from reproducing with each other is called reinforcement. When is reinforcement beneficial?
Option 1: When populations would otherwise fuse over time
Option 2: When hybrids have lower fitness than either parent population
Option 3: When prezygotic isolating mechanisms are in place
Option 4: When one population could be overwhelmed by the other
Option 5: Reinforcement is beneficial under all of the above conditions

Question Text: Imagine two North American ground cricket species that contact each other... These two species do not mate much due to differences in their courtship songs. The northern cricket does not seem to tolerate the warmer temperatures well... Which is the most likely outcome?
Option 1: Fusion of the populations
Option 3: Hybrid zone formation
Option 4: Extinction of one of the species

Question Text: Which of the following evolutionary processes is not acting when two groups of organisms are identified as different species according to the biological species concept?
Option 1: Genetic drift
Option 3: Mutation

Question Text: Which of the following reproductive barriers would prevent a mating attempt?
Option 1: Behavioural isolation
Option 3: Hybrid sterility

Question Text: Which of the following reproductive barriers would prevent a hybrid zygote from developing into a viable fertile adult?
Option 1: Behavioural isolation
Option 3: Mechanical isolation

Question Text: You are studying two populations of spiders of the Dolomedes genus... One population lives in a pond while another nearby population lives in the trees. Which of the following species concept would you be using if you determined that these were two different species?
Option 1: Biological
Option 3: Phylogenetic

Question Text: Which of the following is a disadvantage of the morphological species concept?
Option 1: It cannot be used to evaluate fossils.
Option 2: It is subjective in nature with differences of opinion that cannot be resolved by data.
Option 3: Two groups may use very similar resources, but be evolutionarily independent.
Option 4: Reliable information exists only for a small number of organisms.

Question Text: Six potential species of seaside sparrows were genetically analyzed... If you could only save two subspecies of the Seaside Sparrows from extinction would you choose two subspecies from the Atlantic Coast two from the Gulf Coast or one each from the Atlantic and Gulf Coasts?
Option 1: Save one each from the Atlantic and Gulf Coasts to preserve more genetic diversity.
Option 2: Save two subspecies from the Atlantic Coast, as this coastline is longer and more likely to provide habitat for the birds.
Option 3: Save two subspecies from the Gulf Coast, as this area is much warmer and birds are more likely to survive.

Question Text: Which of the following is "FALSE regarding speciation?
Option 1: Once two groups have become physically isolated, natural selection deliberately isolates them reproductively.
Option 2: Speciation can occur among individuals occupying the same geographic area.
Option 3: Polyploidy leads to rapid speciation relative to allopatric speciation.
Option 4: When populations come back into contact, a hybrid zone is where interbreeding may occur.

Question Text: A population of ground squirrels is separated by a glacier. Changes in which of the following would most likely result in reproductive isolation if the two populations came into contact several generations later?
Option 1: A change in the fur colour of one group.
Option 2: A change in the diet of one group.
Option 3: A change in the gene encoding of a receptor on the egg-cell surface in one group.
Option 4: A change in the alarm call of one group to warn of predators.

Question Text: A species of groundhog spreads through an environmentally varying region... the different populations... accumulate habitat-specific morphological traits. Which of the following is this an example of?
Option 1: Allopatric speciation
Option 3: Species cluster

Question Text: Several stickleback species live in sympatry... Occasionally the two kinds mate resulting in hybrid offspring. Which of the following is a potential reason for maintenance of these two separate groups despite the occasional hybridization event?
Option 1: Hybrids share traits of both species and thus are specialized and able to hunt well in either environment.
Option 2: Hybrids share traits of both species and thus are able to attract mates of either species.
Option 3: Hybrids share traits of both species and thus are not specialized to either environment in terms of hunting.
Option 4: Hybrids share traits of both species and thus may not have specific traits to attract mates of either species.

Question Text: The color code in this model represents genotypes.
This model shows that evolution is occurring.
This model shows the origin of a new allele via mutation.
The allele frequencies of moss on the taller mountain are 0.86A and 0.14a.

Two populations of birds with somewhat different coloration live on opposite sides of a peninsula. The habitat between the populations is not suitable for these birds. When birds from the two populations are brought together they produce young whose appearance is intermediate between the two parents... What keeps the two populations separate?
Option 1: Temporal reproductive isolation
Option 3: Behavioral reproductive isolation

Question Text: Dog breeders maintain the purity of breeds by keeping dogs of different breeds apart when they are fertile. This kind of isolation is most similar to which of the following reproductive isolating mechanisms?
Option 1: Temporal isolation
Option 3: Habitat isolation

Question Text: Rocky Mountain juniper and one-seeded juniper have overlapping ranges... Pollen grains from one species are unable to germinate... on the female ovules of the other species. These two juniper species are kept separated by _______.
Option 1: habitat isolation
Option 3: gametic isolation

Question Text: On the Bahamian island of Andros mosquitofish populations live in various... ponds... When placed together in the same body of water the two kinds of female mosquitofish exhibit exclusive breeding preferences for males that swim in the same manner. Which type of reproductive isolation operates...?
Option 1: Behavioral isolation
Option 3: Gametic isolation

Question Text: Three populations of crickets look very similar but the males have courtship songs that sound different. What function would this difference in song likely serve if the populations came in contact?
Option 1: A temporal reproductive isolating mechanism
Option 3: A behavioral reproductive isolating mechanism

Question Text: Two hypothetical species of sparrow A and B overwinter together in mixed flocks in Costa Rica... What can you say about the isolating mechanisms of these two species?
Option 1: They must have strong postzygotic isolating mechanisms to spend winter in such close proximity.
Option 2: They must have strong prezygotic isolating mechanisms to spend winter in such close proximity.
Option 3: The winter habitat has no bearing on their degree of reproductive isolation.
Option 4: Reinforcement must be occurring when they winter together.

Question Text: The common edible frog of Europe is a hybrid... Both male and female hybrids exist but when they mate... they are rarely successful in producing offspring. What can you infer from this information?
Option 1: Postzygotic isolation exists between the two frog species.
Option 2: Prezygotic isolation exists between the two frog species.
Option 3: These two species are likely in the process of fusing back into one species.
Option 4: The hybrids form a separate species under the biological species concept.

Question Text: Two populations of birds... live on opposite sides of a peninsula... When birds from the two populations are brought together they produce young whose appearance is intermediate... The two populations are _______.
Option 1: different subspecies under the morphological species concept
Option 2: different species under the biological species concept
Option 3: different species under the phylogenetic species concept

Question Text: Refer to the accompanying figure. Which of the following forms a monophyletic group?
Option 1: A, B, C, and D
Option 3: D, E, and F

Question Text: The peppered moth... The dark- and light-colored forms readily interbreed with each other... Should the two forms of moths be considered separate species?
Option 1: Yes because natural selection has affected the frequency of the two different forms.
Option 2: Yes because they have completely different coloration.
Option 3: Yes, because they have completely different coloration and natural selection has affected the frequency of the two different forms.

Question Text: Researchers have examined species of snapping shrimp on both sides of the isthmus [of Panama]... The sister populations on opposite sides of the isthmus are true species under which species concept?
Option 1: The biological species concept
Option 3: The morphological species, biological species, and phylogenetic species concepts

Question Text: Which of the following statements about species as defined by the biological species concept is (are) correct? (I. Defined by reproductive isolation II. Used for extinct forms III. All members can potentially interbreed)
Option 1: I and II
Option 3: II and III

Question Text: What does the biological species concept use as the primary criterion for determining species boundaries?
Option 1: Geographic isolation
Option 3: Gene flow

Question Text: On the volcanic equatorial West African island of São Tomé two species of fruit fly exist... Which of the following reduces gene flow between the gene pools of the two species on São Tomé despite the existence of hybrids?
Option 1: Gametic isolation
Option 3: Hybrid sterility

Question Text: On the volcanic equatorial West African island of São Tomé two species of fruit fly exist... The observation that island D. yakuba is more closely related to mainland D. yakuba than island D. yakuba is to D. santomea is best explained by proposing that D. santomea _______.
Option 1: descended from a now-extinct non-African fruit fly
Option 2: arrived on the island before D. yakuba
Option 3: descended from a single colony of D. yakuba which had been introduced from elsewhere with no subsequent colonization events
Option 4: descended from an original colony of D. yakuba, of which there are no surviving members; the current island D. yakuba represents a second colonization event

Question Text: Males of different species of the fruit fly Drosophila that live in the same parts of the Hawaiian Islands have different elaborate courtship rituals. What type of reproductive isolation does this represent?
Option 1: Habitat isolation
Option 3: Behavioral isolation

Question Text: Bird guides once listed the myrtle warbler and Audubon’s warbler as distinct species. Recently these birds have been classified as... the yellow-rumped warbler. Which of the following pieces of evidence if TRUE would be cause for this reclassification?
Option 1: The two forms interbreed often in nature, and their offspring have good survival and reproduction.
Option 2: The two forms live in similar habitats.
Option 3: The two forms have many genes in common.
Option 4: The two forms are very similar in coloration.

Question Text: The Isthmus of Panama... There are currently two large permanent bridges that span the Panama Canal... If snapping shrimp avoid swimming at night and avoid swimming under shadows then what do these bridges represent for the snapping shrimp?
Option 1: Sources of refuge
Option 3: Sources of a hybrid zone between the two bridges

Question Text: In a remote region a river system once united a diverse population of a single fish species. However... a massive land formation named Mount Aquarius emerged and divided the river... How does the emergence of Mount Aquarius contribute to allopatric speciation in fish in this scenario?
Option 1: Mount Aquarius enhances gene flow between fish populations.
Option 2: Mount Aquarius facilitates interbreeding between fish populations.
Option 3: Mount Aquarius acts as a physical barrier, leading to the isolation of fish populations.
Option 4: Mount Aquarius has no impact on fish speciation.

Question Text: Explain allopatric speciation by vicariance in the context of the emergence of Mount Aquarius.
Option 1: Allopatric speciation occurs when two fish populations are geographically separated, as seen with the emergence of Mount Aquarius, leading to the formation of a new species.
Option 2: Allopatric speciation occurs when two fish populations share the same habitat, leading to the formation of a new species.
Option 3: Allopatric speciation occurs when two fish populations are isolated due to behavioral differences.
Option 4: Allopatric speciation occurs when two fish populations interbreed, leading to the formation of a new species.

Question Text: Which of the following describes the most likely order of events in allopatric speciation?
Option 1: Genetic drift, genetic isolation, and divergence
Option 2: Genetic isolation, divergence, and genetic drift
Option 3: Divergence, genetic drift, and genetic isolation
Option 4: Divergence, genetic isolation, and genetic drift
Option 5: Genetic isolation, genetic drift, and divergence

Question Text: Which factors are often associated with speciation events in island-dwelling species such as the Galápagos finches?
Option 1: Dispersal and colonization
Option 3: Mutation and dispersal

Question Text: In a vast lake ecosystem... Recently a unique microhabitat has developed... If speciation occurs what type of speciation and how?
Option 1: The evolution of new fish species due to allopatric speciation by vicariance.
Option 2: The evolution of new fish species within the same geographical area, due to sympatric speciation.
Option 3: The evolution of new species through sexual selection.
Option 4: The evolution of species through allopatric speciation by dispersal.

Question Text: In a vast lake ecosystem... Recently a unique microhabitat has developed... How does this microhabitat likely contribute to speciation in the fish population?
Option 1: The microhabitat limits gene flow between fish populations.
Option 2: The microhabitat acts as a physical barrier, leading to the isolation of fish populations.
Option 3: The microhabitat facilitates interbreeding between fish populations.
Option 4: The microhabitat has no impact on fish speciation.

Question Text: There is an island in the middle of a large river that houses a large population of ants. Damming of the river causes the island to flood... The ants cannot swim so are now in multiple isolated populations. Which of the following best describes this event?
Option 1: This situation represents isolation by dispersal.
Option 2: This situation represents isolation by vicariance.
Option 3: This situation represents a form of sympatric speciation.
Option 4: Only a rare development of polyploidy could lead to speciation in this case.

Question Text: A small number of birds arrive on an island from a neighboring larger island... About twice a year one or two more birds from the neighboring island arrive. These new arrivals _______.
Option 1: speed up the process of speciation
Option 2: tend to promote adaptation to the new food plants
Option 3: tend to slow adaptation to the new food plants

Question Text: Which of the following could be a vicariance event?
Option 1: The level of water in a lake recedes, creating two lakes where there used to be one.
Option 2: Some insects get blown in a storm to a new mountain range, where they lay eggs.
Option 3: Radiation near Chernobyl increases mutation rates, causing an increase in autopolyploidy.
Option 4: Global warming allows populations of mosquitoes to survive at higher elevations.

Question Text: Which of the following does NOT tend to promote speciation?
Option 1: The founder effect
Option 3: Natural selection

Question Text: House finches... If the two forms were unable to interbreed when their expanding ranges met it would be an example of _______.
Option 1: prezygotic isolation
Option 3: allopatric speciation

Question Text: Most causes of speciation are relatively slow in that they may take many generations of changes with the exception of _______.
Option 1: polyploidy
Option 3: colonization

Question Text: Two researchers experimentally formed tetraploid frogs... If this type of tetraploid formed in the wild what would be the result?
Option 1: The two parent species would interbreed and fuse into one species.
Option 2: The two parent species would recognize each other as mates.
Option 3: The tetraploids would be reproductively isolated from both parent species.
Option 4: The tetraploids would be selected against.

Question Text: Why is speciation by polyploidy more likely in plants than in animals?
Option 1: Plant gametes can be produced by meiosis from somatic cells that have undergone many rounds of mitosis.
Option 2: Plant gametes lack postzygotic isolating mechanisms.
Option 3: Plants are sessile and therefore are never prezygotically isolated to ensure reproduction.
Option 4: Plants are sessile and cannot speciate via dispersal.
Option 5: Plants lack the DNA repair enzymes that animals have.

Question Text: Which of the following statements explains why animals are less likely than plants to speciate by polyploidy?
Option 1: Animals self-fertilize less often than plants, so diploid gametes are less likely to fuse.
Option 2: Animals have better mechanisms for repairing chromosomes than plants have.
Option 3: Animals are more mobile, so they have more effective prezygotic isolating mechanisms.
Option 4: Animals are more mobile, so populations get separated far less often.
Option 5: Animals use a more rigorous form of meiosis than plants making diploid gametes much more difficult to form.

Question Text: A researcher notices that in a certain moth species some females prefer to feed and lay eggs on domesticated solanaceous plants... Other females prefer to feed and lay eggs on wild solanaceous plants... What might be occurring in this moth species?
Option 1: Divergence in sympatry
Option 3: Postzygotic isolation

Question Text: In a hypothetical situation a certain species of flea feeds only on pronghorn antelopes... Some of these fleas develop a strong preference for cattle blood... If this situation persists and new species evolve this would be an example of _______.
Option 1: sympatric speciation and habitat isolation
Option 3: allopatric speciation and habitat isolation

Question Text: Beetle pollinators of a particular plant are attracted to its flowers’ bright orange color... A mutant version of the plant with red flowers becomes more common... A particular variant of the beetle prefers the red flowers... What kind of speciation has occurred...?
Option 1: Allopatric speciation; temporal isolation
Option 3: Allopatric speciation; behavioral isolation

Question Text: The origin of a new plant species by hybridizing two existing species coupled with accidents during cell division is an example of _______.
Option 1: allopatric speciation and autopolyploidy
Option 3: allopatric speciation and allopolyploidy

Question Text: Plant species A has a diploid number of 12. Plant species B has a diploid number of 16. A new species C arises as an allopolyploid from A and B. The diploid number for species C would probably be _______.
Male frogs give calls that attract female frogs... If reinforcement is occurring what would you expect if you compare the calls of the two species in zones of sympatry versus zones of allopatry?
Option 1: Calls would be more similar in areas of sympatry.
Option 2: Calls would be more different in areas of sympatry.
Option 3: None of the above outcomes would be expected.

Question Text: Male frogs give calls that attract female frogs... What outcomes could possibly occur where the ranges of two species overlap? (I. Fusion II. Stable hybrid zone III. Divergence)
Option 1: I
Option 3: III

Question Text: Reinforcement is most likely to occur when _______.
Option 1: the environment is changing
Option 2: hybrids have lower fitness than either parent population
Option 3: prezygotic isolating mechanisms are in place

Question Text: The phenomenon of fusion is likely to occur when after a period of geographic isolation two populations meet again and _______.
Option 1: their chromosomes are no longer homologous enough to permit meiosis
Option 2: an increasing number of infertile hybrids is produced over the course of the next 100 generations
Option 3: no reproduction occurs in the hybrid zone
Option 4: an increasing number of viable fertile hybrids is produced over the course of the next 100 generations

Question Text: A hybrid zone is properly defined as _______.
Option 1: an area where the ranges of two closely related species overlap, but do not interbreed
Option 2: an area where mating occurs between members of two closely related species, producing viable offspring
Option 3: a zone where sterile hybrids form, kept separate by postzygotic barriers
Option 4: an area where members of two closely related species intermingle, but gene flow is prevented by prezygotic barriers

Question Text: On the volcanic equatorial West African island of São Tomé two species of fruit fly exist... If the low number of hybrid flies in the hybrid zone... is due to the fact that hybrids are poorly adapted... these conditions will most likely lead to _______.
Option 1: fusion
Option 3: stability

Question Text: On the Bahamian island of Andros mosquitofish populations live in various... ponds... What is the best way to promote fusion between two related populations of mosquitofish...?
Option 1: Build a canal linking the two ponds that permits free movement of mosquitofish, but not of predators.
Option 2: Transfer only female mosquitofish from a predator-rich pond to a predator-poor pond.
Option 3: Perform a reciprocal transfer of females between predator-rich and predator-poor ponds.
Option 4: Remove predators from a predator-rich pond and transfer them to a predator-poor pond.

Question Text: Interpret this phylogenetic tree of several trumpeter species... P. interjecta and P. dextralis are both descendants of a common ancestor symbolized by the area marked 3.
Interpret this phylogenetic tree... The areas marked 2 and 3 are called nodes.
Interpret this phylogenetic tree... The areas marked 2 and 3 are called forks.
Interpret this phylogenetic tree... The vertical line marked 1 shows that speciation occurred very rapidly.
Interpret this phylogenetic tree... A speciation event occurred at the area marked 2.
Interpret this phylogenetic tree... The birds represented by the area marked 3 belong to the species P. obscura.
Which of the following trees represents the same information about speciation rates as in the reference tree...?
Option 1: Tree A
Option 3: Tree C

Question Text: The approach to estimating phylogenetic trees is most like the approach of which species concept?
Option 1: Morphological species concept
Option 3: Phylogenetic species concept
